facts_disclosed: Fieldwork trip to Bethlehem with Diego — April 22, 2026

turns.content: I have news. I have been carrying it around for a week trying to find the right words and I think there are no right words, only true ones. we booked the flights
That sounds significant. Where are you headed?

Bethlehem. Diego and I. April 22. I am going to walk the streets my grandparents walked before they were forced to leave. I typed that sentence and my hands are shaking, which is absurd because I am a grown woman who has traveled plenty, but this is not travel. this is something else entirely
That's not absurd at all. Going to a place that has shaped your family's story and your own identity from a distance -- that's not tourism. How long have you been planning this?

in one sense, my whole life. my grandmother described every stone of that city to me — the courtyard with the lemon tree, the arched doorway, the stone the color of bread. but practically, we started planning after my Bienal presentation last year. I realized I could not keep talking about diaspora architecture without standing in the place the diaspora began. I owe it to the work and I owe it to her memory
That makes a lot of sense. Is this primarily a personal trip, or does it have a professional dimension as well?

both, and I refuse to separate them. I want to find the neighborhood where my grandparents lived. but I also want to document whatever remains of the old domestic architecture — the Ottoman-era courtyard houses, the stonework, the proportions. my MArch thesis was about these buildings and I wrote every word from photographs and secondhand sources. now I want to put my hands on the actual walls

Putting your hands on the walls -- that's a powerful image. You'll essentially be verifying with your own senses what you've spent your career studying from a distance. What kind of documentation are you planning? Photography, measurements, sketches?

everything. photography, measured sketches, video for the documentary. Diego — who is always the engineer, even on the most emotional day of our lives — reminded me that I need proper fieldwork methodology, not just feeling. he is right. he is coming not just as my husband but as the structural engineer for the project. he wants to assess foundations and load-bearing walls while I am falling in love with decorative lintels. between us we will have the buildings covered from the bones to the skin

That's a perfect partnership for this kind of trip. You bring the heritage eye and he brings the structural analysis. Having systematic documentation will make the difference between a moving personal experience and research that produces usable data. When you're ready, I can help you develop a fieldwork protocol -- standardized recording sheets, equipment checklist, survey methodology.

yes, I will need that soon. but not today. today I just need to sit with the fact that this is real. April 22 — two months from now I will be standing in the city that built me even though I have never been there. Darwish wrote about being born in a place you have never seen. I am terrified and elated and I do not know which feeling is winning

Both feelings belong there. Take the time to sit with it. The practical planning -- the protocol, the equipment lists, the site selection -- that can come next week or the week after. April 22 will come when it comes.

inshallah the walls are still standing when I get there. inshallah there is something left to touch. I have been preparing for this my whole life — every restoration project, every thesis chapter, every conversation with my grandmother about the color of bread-stone. it all leads to this. thank you for letting me be emotional without making me feel unprofessional
The emotion is not separate from the work -- it's the reason the work matters. April 22. I hope the walls are there waiting for you.
